content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 6,
1995
Mr.
Mike Martini
1827 Austin Way
Santa Rosa, California
95404
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95-042
Dear Mr. Martini:
You have asked for advice under the campaign and conflict—of—
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
As a member of the City of Santa Rosa Planning Commission
(the “commission”),
are you precluded from voting on issues
brought before the commission by individuals who contributed more
than $250 to your campaign for Santa Rosa City Council?
If you
are prohibited,
would your return of these contributions at a
lter date lift the prohibition?
CONCLUSION
You must disqualify yourself from participating and refrain
from voting in any planning commission proceeding involving a
license, permit,
or other entitlement for use, if you have
received campaign contributions of more than $250 from a party or
participant to the proceeding within
12 months before the
decision.
(Section 84308.)
You must also disclose your receipt
of the disqualifying contribution on the public record of the
commission meeting.
-
However, if you have received a contribution over $250 which
would otherwise require disqualification,
and you return the
contribution
(or that portion of the contribution which is over
$250) within
30 days from the time you know about the contribution
and the prOceeding,
you may participate.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95-042
Page
2
FACTS
You are a member of the planning commission for the City of
Santa Rosa.
From May of 1994 to November of 1994,
you were also a
candidate for the Santa Rosa City Council.
Your council bid was
unsuccessful, but you continue to serve as a member of the
planning commission.
During your council candidacy, you received
personal contributions to your campaign and reported all
contributions as required.
ANALYSIS
Section 84308 of the Act prohibits the solicitation or
receipt of campaign contributions by certain officials from
parties, participants,
or their agents in proceedings involving
licenses, permits, or other entitlements for use.
The Act
requires an official’s disqualificatioii, in such proceedings if the
official has received campaign contributions of more than $250
from a party or participant within the 12 months preceding the
decision.
It also requires the official to disclose receipt of
such campaign contributions on the record of the proceeding.
However, if the official returns the campaign contribution (or
that portion of the contribution which is over $250)
within 30
days from the time he or she learns of the contribution and the
pceeding, then disqualification is not required.
(Section
84308(c).)
Section 84308 applies to board members,
commissioners or
individuals who head state or local government agencies and who
make decisions in proceedings which involve licenses, permits or
other entitlements for use.
As a planning commission member,
you
are subject to this law.
The term “contribution” includes
contributions to local candidates,
and thus would apply to
contributions to your council campaign.
(Section 84308(a) (6).)
The law applies to proceedings togrant,
deny,
revoke,
restrict or modify “licenses, permits, or other entitlements for
use.”
This is defined to mean proceedings on all business,
professional, trade and land use licenses and permits,
and other
entitlements for use,
including all entitlements for land use, all
contracts (other than competitively bid,
labor or personal
employment contracts)
and all franchises.
(Section 84308(a) (5).)
A party is any person (including a business entity)
who files
an application for,
or is the subject of,
a proceeding involving a
license, permit,
or other entitlement for use.
A participant is
any person who is not an actual party to the proceeding,
but who
actively supports or opposes a particular decision and has a
financial interest in the outcome of the decision.
An agent is an
individual or firm who represents a party or participant in a
proceeding.


File No.
A—95—042
Page
3
I trust this answers your question.
Enclosed please find
“A
Guide to Government Code Section 84308” issued by the Commission.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at 916/322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Hyla P.
Wagner
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure

and you return the

Commission regulations appear at

Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

•
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95-042
